text.heading: Abduction of a monarch
text.p: A revolution consuming its own in the Niger Delta?

When His Royal Highness, Simon Ebebi, the king of Elemebiri, in Ekerema Local Government Area of Bayelsa State was kidnapped in his home last Wednesday, the question being asked was if the Niger Delta revolution is not consuming its children. Pa Ebebi is the father of Bayelsa State Deputy governor, Mr. Peremebowei Ebebi.

Three days before the incident, the government of Bayelsa State entered into a ceasefire agreement with the militants. It is certain now that the agreement is of no effect. The state government ought not to have entered into any agreement with criminal organisations. There are groups that have peacefully and intelligently engaged the Nigerian State on the Niger Delta question; therefore, if there should be any negotiation and agreement, it should have been with such groups. It is well known that criminal gangs operating in the Niger Delta under the façade of'militancy' commit sundry criminal acts to fund their operations and regrettably enjoy state patronage as well.

These criminal gangs engage in economic sabotage by stealing crude and refined fuel from pipelines; engage in armed robbery, especially of banks and kidnap people for ransom. In the past, kidnapping was restricted to foreigners, especially oil workers, but now, the Niger Delta is feeding on itself. The criminals have no sense of chivalry; they do not spare women, children or even the aged.

The mask is off now and the motive behind their activities has been exposed. They have been preying on the misfortune of the Niger Delta to line their own pockets. This is condemnable. It is so sad that their criminality has become the face of the struggles in the Niger Delta.

The pattern of relationship the government of Bayelsa State cultivates with them must change. The first step is to delegitimise them. There should be no pact with criminal organisations. Giving them money is counterproductive. It will reinforce in them the need to continue on the criminal path, since it is profitable.

Secondly, the state must drain the swamp. There is too much indolence in the Niger Delta. Oil is a God-given natural resource, but it takes effort to benefit from it. No doubt the government must invest in people. While we accelerate the demand for greater control and share of the oil resources by the people of Niger Delta, there should be accountability for the little that comes in.

For instance, Bayelsa State collected N8.8 billion from the Federation Account last month. With that modest income, it should be able to provide free and compulsory education at all levels for its citizens without external help. There is a correlation between the criminality going on in the state and its status as an educationally disadvantaged state. When the mind is uncultivated, the devil will surely find good use for it.

Thirdly, the leaders of Niger Delta must also lay good examples for the youth. There is too much looting of public resources by governors and others. The message being passed to the youth is that hardwork and diligence is the wrong path to success. This offers criminal attraction for the youth.

The kidnapping of the deputy governor’s father is a foretaste of the progression of the evils of violent and criminal gangs in the Niger Delta. It will only grow worse if it is not properly addressed now. Throwing money at them is certainly not the solution.
